narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 66% chance you'll struggle to pass the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's mid-range standing around the fifth or sixth decile—as well as an LSAT score of 29, an undergraduate GPA of 2.9, middle family income, Asian racial background, female gender, and plans to attend full-time. These details were compared to averages like LSAT scores around 37 and GPAs near 3.2, placing several of your academic markers below what's common for students who succeed. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your low first-year school decile, which pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure more than any other factor. Right behind that was your third-year decile, also in the bottom rung, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance. Your LSAT score came next in importance, as its below-average level further tilted the outlook negatively. Family income in the middle bracket played a moderate role in the same direction, followed by your racial background, with undergraduate GPA having a smaller but still unfavorable pull, and your full-time status and gender having the least sway. Overall, these elements together painted a riskier picture than for typical applicants who pass the bar, where stronger early grades and test scores dominate success stories. While demographics like gender and race had minimal direct impact here, your academic indicators carried the heaviest weight. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the average or higher, as it's a key factor you might realistically improve with preparation. Building a stronger recent academic record through additional coursework or certifications could also help offset those early low deciles. We encourage you to use this feedback as a roadmap—many students turn things around with targeted effort.
